title: The Auctioneer, by Joan Samson - Book Review
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jgfWTFUl_58

The last-minute substitution that saved October: after two lukewarm reads, Daniel (@dungeondive) reached for a rumoured stone-cold classic — Joan Samson's The Auctioneer (1977), back in print via Valancourt's Paperbacks from Hell line — and found it fantastic. The premise is quiet and terrifying: Perly Dunsmore arrives in a small New Hampshire town as an auctioneer, soliciting 'donations' door to door to fund a bigger police force against the dangerous outside world. Auction by auction, the townsfolk willingly hand over everything they own until they're living in empty houses, policed by the militia their fear paid for. Daniel reads it as an it-can't-happen-here allegory with teeth: we all believe we'd stand up to creeping tyranny, and this book patiently demonstrates why we don't — power isn't taken, it's given. He found himself genuinely angry at the characters' passivity, which is precisely the design: a frog-boiling structure he wishes broke slightly sooner, with a catharsis quieter than the Under the Dome-style crescendo he craved. The saddest note is history's: Samson died of cancer soon after publication, and Daniel mourns the alternate timeline where a woman's voice rose alongside King and Straub in 70s horror. Highly recommended — 'chilling' is the word.

----

The Auctioneer argues that tyranny is given, not taken — and makes you furious at victims for complying. What's the most politically unsettling horror novel you've read, and did its anger work on you?
